title: What is Hierarchical Classification?
concept: Hierarchical classification structures classes in a tree or graph topology, reflecting nested relationships rather than flat lists.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main challenge when distinguishing extremely similar sub-classes like different dog breeds?

options.text: Low inter-class variance (different breeds look almost identical)

exercises.explain: Correct! Visually similar sub-classes have very low inter-class variance, making them hard to tell apart.

cards.title: Sorting the natural world
cards.body: If you see a retriever, it is not just a 'golden retriever'. It is also a 'dog', a 'canine', a 'mammal', and an 'animal'. These categories are nested inside each other. Hierarchical classification models this structured reality.

cards.title: What is Hierarchical?
cards.body: Hierarchical classification is a classification task where categories are organized in a structured tree or graph taxonomy. Predictions must respect parent-child relationships (e.g., if a model predicts an object is a 'Husky', it must also predict it is a 'Dog').

cards.title: Computer folder structures
cards.body: Think of folders on your computer. You might have a folder named Photos, inside it Vacation, and inside that Hawaii. A file in the Hawaii folder is automatically inside the Vacation folder and the Photos folder. They are not independent folders scattered on your desktop.

cards.title: Vehicle taxonomy
cards.body: Instead of predicting from a flat list like [Sedan, SUV, Yacht, Cargo_Ship], a hierarchical model first classifies Land Vehicle vs. Water Vehicle. Under Land Vehicle, it splits into Car vs. Truck, and then down to specific models.

cards.title: Flat lists lose information
cards.body: You might think treating all classes as a flat list of 1,000 choices is fine. But if a model confuses a 'husky' with a 'wolf', it's a minor mistake. Confusing a 'husky' with a 'banana' is a major mistake. Flat classifiers treat both mistakes as equal; hierarchical classifiers know the difference.

left.text:
List: [Husky, Pug, Eagle, Falcon]
All classes are independent
Mistakes are penalized equally

right.text:
Tree: Canine -> [Husky, Pug]
Tree: Raptor -> [Eagle, Falcon]
Relationships are preserved

exercises.prompt: Which of these classifications uses a hierarchical structure?
exercises.explain: A nested progression from general categories to specific ones is the definition of hierarchical classification.

options.text: Classifying an animal as Animal -> Vertebrate -> Mammal -> Carnivore -> Canine -> Dog
Choosing a single tag from the list: [Red, Green, Blue]
Tagging an image with multiple labels: [Sky, Cloud, Sun]
Predicting the price of a house

exercises.prompt: True or false: Flat classification models understand that confusing a tiger with a lion is a better mistake than confusing a tiger with a refrigerator.
exercises.explain: False. Flat models treat all classes as independent, meaning any wrong prediction is penalized exactly the same way.
The structure can be a tree or a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) indicating parent-to-child links.
exercises.sentence: Hierarchical classification structures classes in a tree or ___ graph.

exercises.prompt: Which prediction result demonstrates hierarchical classification structure?
exercises.explain: Predicting along a path from parent to child demonstrates hierarchical structure.

options.text: Prediction path: Category: Vehicle -> Sub-category: Wheeled -> Specific: Truck

exercises.prompt: You are designing a product classifier for an e-commerce giant with 10,000 specific classes. If you train a flat classifier, it will suffer from poor accuracy due to the sheer number of classes. How does a hierarchical approach help?

options.text: It breaks the decision into smaller, easier steps (e.g. first choosing 'Electronics' vs. 'Clothing', then narrowing down).
It reduces the size of the input image automatically.
It allows the model to bypass convolutional calculations.
It changes the model's loss function to mean squared error.

exercises.explain: Exactly! By breaking a complex 10,000-class problem into structured, localized decisions, the model has a much easier learning task at each node of the hierarchy. Next, let's look at the difference between Trees and DAGs!
